A legal team asked their AI assistant about a penalty clause.
It answered confidently. Cited specific terms. Sounded completely reasonable.
The clause didn't exist.
This is what happens when you give a powerful LLM a job it wasn't built for answering questions about documents it has never seen.
I've been building and studying RAG systems for document intelligence: policies, contracts, NDAs, compliance manuals and wrote a detailed breakdown of how it actually works under the hood.
Not the buzzword version. The real one.
What's inside: 
→ Why LLMs fail for private documents (and why fine-tuning isn't the fix) 
→ How chunking strategy makes or breaks your retrieval quality 
→ What the LLM actually sees inside the context window 
→ How to evaluate a RAG system with real scoring examples 
→ When RAG is the wrong choice entirely
If you're a PM, architect, or tech lead thinking about building document AI  this is the article I wish existed when I started.

We are looking for experienced engineers with deep design expertise to create challenging, simulation-based design problems for training and evaluating advanced AI models. 🚀 We're Hiring | Business Development Representative — Rxobot Labs (Remote, Full-Time)
📍 United States

At Rxobot Labs, we are redefining what's possible at the intersection of artificial intelligence and business growth. Our flagship platform, Valiz.io, empowers small and mid-sized businesses with enterprise-grade AI advertising capabilities — putting the tools of Fortune 500 marketing teams into the hands of everyday entrepreneurs.

We are scaling fast. And we are looking for Business Development Representatives who are ready to grow with us.

💼 The Opportunity

This is a ground-floor opportunity to join a high-growth AI technology company at a critical inflection point. As a BDR, you will be at the forefront of our commercial expansion — identifying opportunities, building meaningful client relationships, and directly driving the revenue trajectory of a company on the rise.

You will receive structured onboarding and comprehensive on-the-job training. This is not a role where you figure things out alone — we invest in our people from day one and equip you with everything you need to succeed.

📌 What You'll Own

↳ Lead generation and outbound prospecting — calls, virtual presentations, and in-person engagements across your territory
↳ Deliver compelling product demonstrations via Zoom that convert prospects into long-term Valiz.io clients
↳ Manage a full sales cycle end-to-end, from initial outreach through contract close
↳ Maintain a high-quality pipeline with consistent forecasting and CRM discipline
↳ Represent Rxobot Labs as a brand ambassador — with professionalism, energy, and purpose

✅ What We're Looking For

↳ Drive 
↳ Strong interpersonal and communication skills — compelling on a cold call, a Zoom, or face-to-face
↳ A self-starter mindset with the ability to thrive in a remote, performance-driven environment
↳ Coachability and a growth orientation — the right person matters more than a perfect resume
↳ Familiarity with digital advertising platforms (Meta, Google Ads) is a strong advantage

💰 Compensation & Growth Path

We believe in rewarding performance aggressively. Our BDRs begin with a performance-based structure — earning commission from day one with no ceiling on earnings — and a clear, defined path to a full-time salaried position ($60,000 base + commission) upon hitting quota milestones within 90 days.

Our platform plans run monthly and/or annually  — recurring revenue that compounds your earnings over time. High performers here do not wait years for recognition. We promote from within and build careers, not just jobs.

📩 Apply via LinkedIn or send your resume and a brief introduction to careers@rxobotlabs.com

We look forward to connecting.

In today’s competitive hiring landscape across India and the Middle East, skipping thorough employee background verification continues to expose companies to severe financial losses, compliance risks, and reputational damage.

Recent reports and incidents highlight the growing threat ~ and how many cases could have been prevented with proactive screening.

𝗜𝗻𝗱𝗶𝗮  

An EY study (May 2025) analyzing over 1 million pre-employment screenings across 90+ organizations revealed surging employment fraud. In healthcare, 83% of discrepant profiles failed employment checks, with 75% submitting fake documents. Financial services saw 84% of discrepancies from forged salary proofs, inflated compensation, and fake degrees. In IT/ITeS, 45% of red-flagged candidates were moonlighting. Notably, most fraud involved experienced professionals: 96% in healthcare, 88% in financial services, and 79% in IT/ITeS.

IDfy’s 2025 Workforce Risks report showed Indian companies lost over ₹150 crore to employee-led and collusion-driven fraud, with 195,163 suspicious white-collar hires flagged before onboarding ~ nearly one in eight BGV reports turning red.

Banking fraud surged 715% in early 2026, with losses hitting ₹21,367 crore in H1, and nearly 50% linked to internal employees who bypassed weak background checks.

A Genius HRTech survey (early 2026) found 74% of respondents most concerned about fake degrees and falsified credentials, while 77% flagged AI-driven deepfakes and synthetic resumes as escalating threats.

Discrepancy rates in high-growth sectors like IT and BFSI reached 12-13% in H1 FY26, with employment history verification most vulnerable.

𝗠𝗶𝗱𝗱𝗹𝗲 𝗘𝗮𝘀𝘁  

In February 2026, Saudi Arabia’s Oversight and Anti-Corruption Authority (Nazaha) announced arrests in multiple fraud cases totaling over SR 10 million (~$2.6M). These included a PIF-affiliated project manager accepting SR 2.2 million in bribes for contract awards and other public/private sector employees involved in bribery, embezzlement, and illegal document issuance ~ underscoring risks from unverified insider integrity.

With remote/hybrid hiring, global talent pools, and AI making sophisticated fakes easier, one-time checks are insufficient. Continuous, tech-enabled verification (education, employment history, criminal records, references) has become essential risk management.

HR leaders, CHROs, and business owners in India, GCC & East Asia: Make robust background screening your first line of defense. It protects assets, culture, and stakeholder trust ~ not just a compliance checkbox.

**Most Kenyan SMEs lose money to vague contracts—here's how to fix it.**

A contract without clear payment terms is a contract waiting to fail. We've reviewed thousands of agreements across East Africa, and the pattern is stark: 67% of disputes stem from ambiguous clauses, not malice.

Here's what works:

1. **Define payment milestones in writing.** Don't rely on verbal agreements. Specify amounts, dates, and consequences for late payment. The CBK recognizes written evidence in commercial disputes.

2. **Use templates aligned to Kenyan law.** Whether you're hiring, partnering, or selling—your contract must reference the Employment Act 2007, Partnership Act, or relevant legislation. Generic templates create liability.

3. **Include dispute resolution clauses.** Add arbitration or mediation provisions before court. It's faster, cheaper, and keeps business relationships intact.

Your contracts are your safety net. SharkFlow Legal uses AI to generate Kenyan-compliant agreements in minutes—cutting legal costs by 70% while protecting your business.

Don't leave money on the table. Secure your contracts today.

Visit legal.sharkflowltd.com to get started.

High above the city at Salesforce Tower, CEO Joseph Orsborn, founder of Lilypad Learning (the first AI-native operating system for special education) shared how he went from Massachusetts Institute of Technology to founder

At Silicon Valley Fellowship, he walked us through how he built Lilypad.

He grew up in Australia working in medical devices at Abbott. When he came to MIT for his MBA, he met people talking about private equity and startups, things he had no exposure to before. 

Someone with more experience told him to follow a very interesting idea, improving mental health access for kids. That's when it clicked for him. He didn't see himself staying in medical devices, so he used his time at MIT to reflect and commit to building something in mental health.

The early days were hard. He talked to 20 different schools before getting his first yes, an $8,000 contract. He said it felt so exciting at the time. Now Lilypad Learning serves districts across the country and the numbers are way higher.

He highlighted something important about regulated industries like education and healthcare. Historically, you had to earn your way in with services before you could sell software, but AI is completely changing that game. Software is so easy to scale, and with AI you can optimize and create value faster than ever before.

He also talked about hiring. His first engineer was bad. The second one transformed the company. Choosing the right person can truly change an organization. That's something I think about a lot as I work on my projects, bringing that energy of change for the better, like I do in LEAD.

He adviced us to find what we are most passionate about, research all the competitors in that space (what they have in common, how they became successful, how they differentiate). 

He also said to talk to at least 100 people, be a good listener (that's the key to being a good salesperson), develop real relationships, and, don't be afraid of feedback, know how to react, know how to adjust.

Thank you Joe for sharing your journey and being so transparent. These lessons are so inspiring for me as I think about my own path in tech.

> Small Language Models (SLMs): A Small Business's Secret Weapon :

> You've probably heard a lot about AI lately ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini. These are Large Language Models (LLMs) and they're impressive. But they're also expensive, slow and honestly overkill for most small businesses. Enter Small Language Models (SLMs) the leaner, faster, more affordable cousins that are quietly becoming a game-changer for small business owners.

> So What Even Is an SLM?

> Think of a large language model like hiring a Harvard-educated consultant who knows everything about everything. An SLM is more like hiring a sharp specialist who knows your industry really well. It's a compact AI model trained to do specific tasks and it does them really, really well without needing a supercomputer to run.

> Why Should a Small Business Care?

> Running a large AI model is like keeping the lights on in a stadium. SLMs are more like a desk lamp they do the job without the electricity bill. Small businesses can run SLMs on affordable hardware or even on a laptop. No expensive cloud subscriptions, no per-query fees adding up every month.

> Because they're smaller, SLMs respond faster. For a customer-facing chatbot answering questions on your website, speed matters. Nobody wants to wait five seconds for an AI to think about a return policy.

> Here's something most people overlook. With a big cloud-based AI, your data customer info, business documents, internal emails leaves your building. With an SLM, you can run it locally on your own machine. Your data stays yours. For a small accounting firm, a medical practice or a legal office, this isn't just nice to have, it's essential.

> It Can Be Trained on Your Business
> SLM can be fine-tuned on your specific products, your tone of voice, your FAQ documents, your industry jargon. The result is an AI that actually sounds like you and knows your business not a generic bot giving generic answers.

> Real Examples of SLMs at Work:
> A bakery uses an SLM to handle customer order inquiries and custom cake requests via WhatsApp automatically, overnight, without staff.

> A small law firm uses one to draft first versions of standard contracts, saving hours of repetitive typing.

> A local e-commerce shop uses an SLM to auto-generate product descriptions from a simple list of features.

The corporate pyramid is dying.

Real estate figured out what comes next thirty years ago.

I've spent nine years inside a brokerage. Nobody has a boss in the traditional sense. Everyone's an independent contractor operating under a shared brand, sharing some back-office services, competing on the open market but cooperating on specific deals. It's closer to a web than a pyramid.

Every agent is a tiny company of one.

Most industries weren't structured this way because running a company of one required too much administrative overhead. You needed someone for HR, legal, accounting, compliance, scheduling, outreach. One person couldn't carry it.

That constraint is dissolving.

Cambridge researchers ran a simulated auto-industry experiment. Generative AI outperformed human executives on strategic decisions in data-rich environments. What's increasingly automatable isn't the doer at the bottom — it's the coordination layer above them.

Meanwhile corporations have mostly stopped hiring new graduates. A Stanford paper last year documented a 13 percent decline in entry-level hiring for AI-exposed jobs since late 2022. The traditional arc of employee-to-manager-to-executive is getting decommissioned from both ends.

So what replaces it.

My bet is multi-agent systems. In the boring sense. Your calendar agent haggling with someone else's calendar agent. Your contract agent negotiating terms with theirs. Research agents coordinating overnight with strangers' research agents, producing artifacts you review in the morning.

Every professional becomes a small principal with a suite of agents acting on their behalf.

That's basically the real estate model, scaled to every industry.

The part I'm uncertain about is risk concentration. Pyramids exist partly because you need someone accountable when things go wrong. I don't know yet how the co-op web handles that. Reputation markets maybe. Escrow. Something nobody has built yet.

If the pyramid is what comes before, and the web is what comes after, what does anyone mentor a young professional into now?

#FutureOfWork #AIAgents #RealEstate

Today PedaGPU was part of CATIE's system-wide AI classroom showcase. Six presentations across the year. Eleven universities represented across the TAMU System. I'm still sitting with it.

I've been a cinematographer for 25 years. In that time, I've watched the industry hit a major inflection point at least 15 times. Film to HD. Avid's $100K stranglehold broken by a $1K copy of Final Cut Pro. Adobe hiring away the senior FCP developers and launching native 4K in Premiere. 32-bit float. Raw and log. Red. Now physical sets to virtual production. Each time, people predicted the end of Hollywood. Each time, the craft evolved, new specialists emerged, and the people who adapted kept working.

What makes AI feel different isn't the inflection point itself. It's the synchronization. Normally when cinematographers panic about HD, doctors aren't panicking about diagnostic tools, lawyers aren't panicking about contract review, and teachers aren't panicking about ChatGPT. Each field gets its crisis in isolation.

AI broke the isolation. Everyone is in crisis at once. There's no stable ground to point at from somewhere else, because everywhere is the unstable ground right now. The whole floor is vibrating at the same time.

If you're uncomfortable right now, that's not a sign something is wrong with you. It's a sign you're paying attention.

Thomas Kuhn wrote about this in The Structure of Scientific Revolutions. Paradigms don't shift smoothly. They lurch. And the next generation adopts the new paradigm because it's what they learned first. That's who my students are.
PedaGPU is my answer to that moment. It's cloud GPU infrastructure I built on my own time to give students at a regional university on-demand access to professional AI filmmaking tools. Not because AI is the future of filmmaking, but because students graduating today are being born into AI the way my generation was born into digital. The question isn't whether they'll use these tools. It's whether someone teaches them to use them with craft and judgment.
That's what a media production program can do that a tutorial can't.
Adapt or die is just lifelong learning with the stakes made visible. Evolve, or become irrelevant. And if educators go irrelevant on AI, the people who will make the decisions about how it enters classrooms are politicians. We know how that goes. If we're not in the conversation, we don't shape it, and neither do our students.

Grateful to Dr. Rad Homayra and CATIE's AI, Innovative & Emerging Technologies Workgroup for the invitation, to TAMU-CC's EPIC conference for the visibility that made it possible, to Dr. Ahmed Mahdy and the Office of Research and Innovation at TAMU-CC for the sustained support, and to TAMU System for building a showcase that recognized work from outside the usual disciplines.

The inflection point is here. I'd rather teach through it than around it.

We've published our Data & AI Market Report for Q3 FY26 - Here's a summary of what I am seeing across the market right now:

1️⃣ The market is tightening, not shrinking. Demand for Data & AI Engineers remains high, but hiring has become slower, more selective, and heavily tied to clear commercial outcomes.
2️⃣ Execution over exploration. Organisations want production-ready AI talent who can deploy and maintain models. We've passed "research" phase.  
3️⃣ Poor data foundations are the silent killer of AI programs. Companies are hiring AI Engineers before fixing their data. The gap between AI ambition and data readiness is where most programs fall over.
4️⃣ The title "AI Engineer" means nothing right now. It's being used interchangeably across data science, machine learning and data engineering, creating confusion and misaligned expectations on both sides of the table.
5️⃣ Databricks & Snowflake talent is a war zone! 80%+ of companies are chasing the same type engineers who simply aren't applying to jobs - they're already employed, well-paid and a job ad won't find them, you need a head-hunter.
6️⃣ The "unicorn" mindset is damaging hiring outcomes, overly specific role requirements are creating longer processes, smaller talent pools and worse results than simply hiring for potential and upskilling. No one knows everything in this day and age.
7️⃣ Onsite vs WFH, companies mandating 3/4 days onsite are significantly shrinking their talent pool. Flexibility isn't a perk anymore. It's one of the first questions candidates are asking.
8️⃣ The contractor market is strengthening. Organisations are opting for contract talent before committing to permanent headcount, and candidates are following suit. The mindset that a permanent role gives more security is near non-existent these days with redundancies being thrown around left, right and centre.

If you want a copy of the report in full, drop me a DM or comment REPORT in the comments.

For me, design has never been about just "making things pretty."

I entered UX and Product Design because I saw it as a bridge. I didn’t want to just move pixels; I wanted to move metrics. I wanted to see how a thoughtful interface could directly impact the bottom line.

Today, that philosophy is no longer just an opinion. It’s the new reality.
With AI tools now generating high-fidelity mockups in seconds, the manual "craft" is becoming a commodity. The bar has shifted. The industry doesn't just need designers who can execute; it needs designers who can strategize.

This shift is actually an incredible unlock. It frees us to focus on what truly matters:

- Solving complex business problems
- Driving measurable outcomes
- Connecting user needs to growth

I’ve always believed that the best design works as hard for the business as it does for the user. It feels like the world is moving in that direction at speed and I couldn't be more excited.

Today's Ottawa Construction Association (O.C.A) event was the perfect balance of speakers and opportunity for relationship building.

Jennie West-Correia, CPF, CEC, reminded us that Construction is a relationship industry… and too many people still treat networking like a transaction. What if we reframed our approach from “what can I get?” to “what can I give?”, AND, at the end of any interaction - repeat someone's name and shake their hand. Thanks Jennie - used that tip all day!

The General Contractors Leadership Panel with Mathew Baranowski, P.Eng., MBA, Shawn Berube, Chris Markell, P.Eng., P.GSC, LEED® AP, Allessandro Guarna, CPA, CA, highlighted that firms are not alone as they navigate complexities in their businesses and to rely on the OCA team. I also enjoyed their discussion on AI and advice to all businesses to set up clear governance, assign a lead that will drive AI in your organization and promote access within for your employees as a whole. 

Josée LP (JLP) included HR advice around 80% of employees are disengaged, while 20% are thriving. And you can save nearly half of them with one thing most leaders avoid… A direct conversation. Not a hint or skirting around the issue. Clarity.

And the best part of my day… I ran into someone I’ve been providing career advice - Marie Kenny Rodney  who is actively looking in Ottawa: Civil Engineering degree, strong in project coordination, contracts, QA, cost tracking, fluently bilingual (actually she speaks 5 languages). Currently enrolled in a Construction Project Management course. She’s doing everything right (networking, asking for advice and she takes notes vigorously with excellent follow up around anything you say) . If you’re hiring, you should meet her.

Claude Design launched this week.

It was my turn to be "cooked."

‘Timeline-based motion design’ – my heart skipped.

I had a decent play with it. Wasn't blown away. But I refuse to be one of those people who acts like because it can't beat me today, it never will. That's a dangerous place to get comfortable.

But I also don't subscribe to the idea that every new tool is an industry killer.

There's a lot of fear in disruption. But we only seem to express that fear when it's our own work being disrupted. 

I use AI to help me draft contracts and proposals. You could argue a lawyer is losing work there. But would I be hiring a lawyer as a solo operator in year one? Probably not. 

When you start a business, you try to do everything yourself because the money isn't there yet.

That's where I think tools like Claude Design genuinely do good. 

If a grassroots startup can put together a decent pitch deck without forking out for a designer, that's lowering the barrier to entry. And that's a good thing.

But I strongly believe that any serious business will continue to need experts. Even if those experts are using AI themselves. 

Stay flexible. Keep learning. Be willing to adapt. We’ll be fine.

Lovely lead comment from our editor today. Want to learn more? Contact Claire Middleton. Work with an organisation that has its own views, not simply repeating what paid advertisers tell them.
      
Why FM Needs a New Strategy for the Talent Crisis
It’s been reported on the news this week that there’s been a surprise fall in the UK unemployment rate to 4.9%. Although this might look like a win on paper, for the FM industry, it is a deceptive signal. As many industry observers have noted, this fall is driven less by a surge in hiring and more by a rise in economic inactivity - simply put, there are fewer people actively looking for work. 

The Real-World Squeeze on the Frontline
The FM sector remains one of the UK’s largest employers of essential labour. While the official unemployment rate drops, the reality on the ground is a tightening vice of labour shortages. 
   · The Skills Gap: If the unemployment rate is falling because people are leaving the workforce, the pool of available talent for critical roles - technical engineers, site managers, and specialist cleaners - shrinks even further. 
· Wage Pressure: A tight labour market, even an artificial one, creates upward pressure on wages. In an industry defined by thin margins and long-term fixed-price contracts, this surprise drop in unemployment is a harbinger of further margin erosion. 

From Management to Attraction
The FM industry can no longer afford to be a passive consumer of labor. We must move from managing staff to actively attracting and retaining a shrinking demographic. 
   1.    Investment in Social Value: If people are leaving the workforce due to burnout or health issues, FM providers must differentiate themselves through superior wellbeing strategies and social value that offers more than just a pay cheque. 
2.    Tech-Enabled Productivity: If we cannot find the people, we must empower the ones we have. This employment data highlights the urgent need for investment in IoT and AI-driven maintenance to reduce the reliance on manual "boots on the ground." 
3.    Governance over Compliance: We must ensure our labour governance is robust. Relying on "gig economy" models to fill gaps caused by a tight market increases the risk of modern slavery and compliance failures. 

Conclusion? 
Don’t be fooled by the headline figures. A 4.9% unemployment rate doesn't mean the talent crisis is over; it means the competition for it has just entered a more aggressive phase. For the FM industry to survive 2026, we must stop looking for "available" labour and start building an environment that people actually want to return to work for. 
The "Employment Mirage" is a warning shot: those who fail to innovate their people strategy now will find themselves with buildings they can no longer afford - or find the staff - to maintain. 
 
David Mcloughlin
Simon Taylor
Dan Clark
FM Business Daily

The AGI Dilemma: Are We Building a Productivity Miracle or a Demand Collapse?

If we get Artificial General Intelligence wired into today’s economics and politics, the dystopia isn’t sci‑fi it’s just the logical conclusion of our current incentives.

We get ultra productive machines owned by a tiny minority, mass unemployment dressed up as “efficiency,” demand collapse, and a society optimised for margins, not humans. 

Yes, there is another path. But it demands something most of us are still dodging, commercial design that treats social stability and human dignity as first class constraints, not externalities. That means pricing, ownership, and product strategy explicitly centred on human outcomes, not just quarterly EPS.

If we re wire incentives, ownership, and safety nets as fast as we increase capability, AGI could fund a shorter work week, universal economic security, and a saner social contract. Commercial models would have to bake in questions like: What happens to our customers’ workforce? What happens to local communities? Are we compressing drudgery, or hollowing out livelihoods?

But here’s the real problem:
•	Who do we actually trust to redesign that system?
•	Which government is prepared to tax and regulate away its own competitive edge?
•	Which board will voluntarily blunt its AGI advantage in the name of social resilience?

We’re sleepwalking into a geo political prisoner’s dilemma: everyone knows we need human centred guardrails and redistribution, but no one wants to be the first to blink and “lose” the AGI race.

So my question to you, as leaders, investors, operators:
If you knew AGI at status quo settings leads to a slow motion collapse of work, demand, and legitimacy, what would you be willing to build differently in your pricing, your product, your hiring plans to avoid that?

And if the honest answer is “nothing,” then we should stop pretending we’re excited about AGI itself, and start admitting we’re excited about being on the winning side of a lose lose game.

#AI #AGI #FutureOfWork #AIethics #EconomicInequality #SocialContract #Leadership #SaaS #PricingStrategy #Policy #TechForGood #DigitalTransformation #Automation

Layoffs in support of AI agents are very short-sighted IMO. 

We are 60 days into having our AI agent for our revenue org - Ranger. It hasn't replaced anyone, and we don't want it to. We want it to accelerate our already talented team. 

That's exactly what it's doing. 

Ranger has provided incredibly valuable support. As a scaling company, our wishlist for talent is long, and our budget is appropriately aligned to our scale point and attainment of our goals. Like most orgs, we are pushing the boundary of resource requests and appropriate budget limitations. 

Enter Ranger. Ranger is already filling the gaps between our goals and what our budget will afford. I see Ranger as our hiring offset. It fills the roles that didn't make it into the budget but will accelerate the work of our team. 

Today, Ranger serves in two key roles that we would not be able to hire this year. It serves as a Director of Sales and as a RevOps Analyst. 

We have made progress on enablement materials, frameworks, and rubrics that kept getting pushed down the priority list. It bridges the listening gaps on calls so I can enter AE 1:1s with insight into their prior week's calls and coaching opportunities within. It solves 40-60% of my requests for analysis throughout our entire GTM. Now I can work with our RevOps leader on more strategic work and bigger initiatives. That's simply how I use it. Our GTM org is finding new use cases every day. 

We are only scratching the surface, but I see a very bright future where AI improves the work and strategic impact of every single person on our team. I see a future where we keep looking to Ranger to offset where we don't have budget resources to fulfill our hiring wishlist. 

Next up with Ranger: 
-Deal desk and initial contract reviews - speeding approval steps
-Links to more marketing systems for full funnel insights - providing more RevOps support to marketing
-Marketing content frameworks so content and nurture strategies can be recommended in deal analysis outputs - pushing opportunities for our team to further collaborate in ABM and improving the utilization of the really great content being created by our team
-Internal GTM LMS development for product and industry-related learning
-Hand-off and knowledge transfer processes with Customer Success
-Sales material development, including business case frameworks and presentations

Hiring in 2026 isn’t slowing down- it’s evolving. Here’s a snapshot of what we are seeing in today’s job market (April):

Growth continues, but with caution: Companies are still adding jobs, just at a slower, more deliberate pace. Hiring hasn’t stopped; don't believe the hype!  However, it’s become more selective.

A divided market is taking shape: Demand remains strong in healthcare, construction, and infrastructure, while tech and many white-collar roles are seeing tighter, more specialized hiring.

Shifts, not shrinkage: Layoffs and hiring are happening simultaneously. Roles aren’t vanishing. Rather, they’re being redistributed across industries.

The skills gap persists: Even with slower hiring, employers continue to struggle to find talent in skilled trades, healthcare, and technical infrastructure.

AI is reshaping hiring, not replacing it: Proficiency in AI is increasingly setting candidates apart in the hiring process.

Reduced job mobility: Workers are staying in roles longer, while employers rely more on contract and temporary positions.

Bottom line: This isn’t a weak job market; it’s a more selective, skills-focused one. Adaptability matters more than ever, and specialized skills are outweighing broad experience.

Elevate Digital

We're hiring: Product Engineer, AI Agent Orchestration.

This is not a traditional software engineering role. Our agents write the code. We need the human in the middle.

Claude, Codex, and Gemini implement the work. Our in-house Hermes orchestration layer coordinates them end-to-end. We're hiring someone to sit between the business and the agents — turning customer conversations into stories and sprints the orchestrator can execute, and continuously sharpening the skills, context, and prompts that drive agent quality.

Reports to: CTO
Seniority: Mid to Senior
Engagement: CONTRACT ONLY (initially)
Work authorization: Must be authorized to work in the US. No visa sponsorship or transfers, but Near Shore candidates are ok (but must be English proficient)

Note: Direct candidates only. No staffing firms, recruiters, or agencies — these will be ignored.
Stack (for context, not a filter): Java / Spring Boot, React/TypeScript, AWS

You'll own both sides of the bridge:

— The product side. Talk to customers. Turn messy needs into clear stories the agents can execute. Decide what the agents work on, in what order, with what context. Close the loop on whether we actually delivered what was needed.

— The agent side. Curate the skills library. Engineer the context agents receive. Grow a point of view on Claude, Codex, and Gemini. Diagnose agent failures at the right layer — model, prompt, context, tool, or orchestrator — and fix them there.

What we're looking for:
• Several years as a working software engineer (any stack)
• Real, hands-on experience with at least one modern coding agent — Claude Code, Codex, Gemini CLI, Cursor, or similar — with opinions about skills, context, subagents, and tools
• Obvious curiosity to go deeper across the rest of the agent ecosystem
• Strong writing — your stories, prompts, and skills ARE your deliverables
• Product sensibility; prior PM titles are NOT expected, you'll grow into this side

What we don't care about:
• Your specific prior stack
• LeetCode
• How much code you personally ship per week
• Whether you already know every frontier model

What this role is NOT:
If you enjoy writing production code more than orchestrating the agents that do, this will frustrate you.

This is one of the most interesting seats in software right now. If it resonates and you meet the criteria above,  email to raju@bookmarked.com.

Hi Friends,

I'm looking for my next full-time product role and hoping my network can help.

Over the last year I've been co-building Hux Partners, an AI-powered analytics platform serving CPG suppliers, tracking over $300M in annual client revenue. I helped build the production data pipeline from scratch using Claude Code and Playwright, led our pivot from contract work to multi-tenant SaaS, and own the full product vision. It's been one of the most rewarding things I've worked on. I'm not walking away from it. I'm transitioning it to a side project so I can find a full-time team to be part of again.

Before that, I was the sole Product Owner at J.B. Hunt for a contractual pricing system supporting billions in annual freight revenue across 50,000+ daily transactions. Zero downtime tolerance, 12 engineers, the whole deal. I left that role with a 55% reduction in support tickets, 27% improvement in release quality, and a few good stories about stopping sprints mid-cycle when the data said we were building the wrong thing.

I'm a PM who actually builds things. I use AI tools daily in production, write requirements engineers don't throw out, and I care a lot about getting the product decision right before the code gets written.

What I'm looking for:
-Product Manager or Product Owner
-Remote or hybrid in Salt Lake City
-B2B SaaS, AI, data platforms, operations tooling
-A team that moves fast and values ownership

If you know of a role, a company, or a person I should talk to, please reach out. Happy to hop on a call, grab coffee if you're local, or just connect here. Tag someone, share this, whatever feels right. I genuinely appreciate it.

🚀 Hiring: Power Platform + Copilot Studio Governance Engineer (#Forcecraver)

We are looking for a highly experienced Power Platform + Copilot Studio Governance Engineer to lead enterprise-scale governance, security, and AI adoption strategies across the Microsoft ecosystem.

This is a strategic, architect-level role focused on building secure, scalable, and compliant Copilot & Power Platform environments.

⸻

📌 Role Details

* Experience: 10+ Years
* Location: Remote
* Employment Type: Contract / Full-Time
* Eligibility: Candidates must be based in India 🇮🇳

⸻

💼 Key Responsibilities

* Define and implement Power Platform & Copilot Studio governance frameworks
* Design environment strategy (Dev/Test/Prod) and lifecycle management
* Enforce DLP policies, security controls, and compliance standards
* Govern Copilot Studio agents and GenAI usage with enterprise guardrails
* Lead Power Platform Center of Excellence (CoE) initiatives
* Manage tenant-level governance, licensing, and access policies
* Implement ALM & CI/CD pipelines for Power Platform solutions
* Ensure data privacy, auditability, and responsible AI practices

⸻

🧠 Required Skills

* Power Apps (Canvas & Model-driven)
* Power Automate
* Copilot Studio (Agent design & governance)
* Dataverse & connectors
* Power Platform Admin Center
* Governance: DLP, environment strategy, compliance

⸻

⭐ Good to Have

* Azure (Azure OpenAI, Entra ID, API Management)
* DevOps & CI/CD pipelines
* Prompt Engineering & GenAI controls
* Enterprise architecture experience

⸻

🎯 Ideal Candidate

* Strong background in Power Platform Architecture + Governance
* Experience driving enterprise AI adoption
* Hands-on with security, compliance, and scalable platform design

⸻

📩 Please reach out to me at +919899634637 or jbhatia@forcecraver.com to discuss further.
🔔 Follow @Jitender Bhatia @Forcecraver Technologies for more job updates.

🚀 Hiring: Staff AI/ML Full Stack Engineer (Contract)
#Forcecraver #Remote #AI #ML #AWS #Databricks #Hiring

We are looking for a Staff AI/ML Full Stack Engineer to lead the design, architecture, and delivery of enterprise-grade AI systems. This is a highly strategic and hands-on role combining full stack development, cloud architecture, and ML engineering.

🔹 Experience: 10+ Years (Min 5+ Years in AI/ML & Cloud)
🔹 Employment Type: 12-Month Contract (Full-Time – 40 hrs/week)
🔹 Location: Remote (India Candidates Only)
🔹 Shift: Mon–Fri | 9:00 AM – 5:30 PM CST

⸻

🔹 Key Responsibilities:

• Lead architecture & design of scalable AI/ML systems
• Build full stack apps using React/Vue/Angular + Python/Golang/Rust
• Develop AI pipelines (ML + LLM + RAG systems)
• Architect cloud-native solutions on AWS & Databricks
• Implement MLOps pipelines, CI/CD, and infra as code
• Design APIs (REST/GraphQL) and microservices
• Drive performance optimization & scalability
• Mentor teams & enforce engineering best practices

⸻

🔹 Mandatory Skills:

✔ Full Stack Development
✔ AWS + Databricks (MLOps deployment)
✔ AI/ML pipelines (LLMs, RAG, embeddings)
✔ Kubernetes, Docker, CI/CD
✔ Strong system design & distributed systems knowledge

⸻

🔹 Preferred Skills:

• Kafka / RabbitMQ / NATS
• OAuth2, JWT, SSO
• Prometheus, Grafana, OpenTelemetry
• Golang / Rust
• SaaS / Enterprise platform experience

⸻

🔹 What We’re Looking For:

A hands-on technical leader who can design and deploy end-to-end AI/ML systems from data ingestion to production, while mentoring teams and driving innovation.

⸻

📩 Please reach out to me at +919899634637 or jbhatia@forcecraver.com to discuss further.
🔔 Follow @Jitender Bhatia @Forcecraver Technologies for more job updates.
